selection.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?

And how?","opinion":"The gender gap in science is not a flaw in the system, but a reflection of how society has shaped expectations—especially for young women. I’ve spent my life as a plumber in Washington DC, working hard side by side with men, fixing pipes and heating systems, often in places where people don’t expect to see a woman. My parents both had only primary education, and yet they taught me that effort and hands-on work matter more than labels. I never saw science as something only for boys. When I was a child, I would watch my father fix a leaky faucet and ask, 'How does it work?' That curiosity was never discouraged. Yet, in school, I saw how girls were often steered toward math and reading, while boys were given tools, experiments, and encouragement to explore. The real gap isn’t in ability—it’s in opportunity and belief. Should we address it? Absolutely. But not through grand policies alone. We need to start in homes and schools, where children are shaped by stories and role models. I’ve seen young women in my neighborhood now joining science fairs, building robots and inventing gadgets—proof that with encouragement, they can thrive. My own hobbies, like yoga and video games, show that women can enjoy both quiet reflection and intense focus. The science world needs more diverse voices, not because of gender, but because of experience. We must simply stop pretending that science is a male-only domain. It’s time to let every child, regardless of gender, see themselves as capable and curious. response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion: The gender gap in science is not a flaw in the system, but a reflection of how society has shaped expectations—especially for young women. I’ve spent my life as a plumber in Washington DC, working hard side by side with men, fixing pipes and heating systems, often in places where people don’t expect to see a woman. My parents both had only primary education, and yet they taught me that effort and hands-on work matter more than labels. I never saw science as something only for boys. When I was a child, I would watch my father fix a leaky faucet and ask, 'How does it work?' That curiosity was never discouraged. Yet, in school, I saw how girls were often steered toward math and reading, while boys were given tools, experiments, and encouragement to explore. The real gap isn’t in ability—it’s in opportunity and belief. Should we address it? Absolutely. But not through grand policies alone. We need to start in homes and schools, where children are shaped by stories and role models. I’ve seen young women in my neighborhood now joining science fairs, building robots and inventing gadgets—proof that with encouragement, they can thrive. My own hobbies, like yoga and video games, show that women can enjoy both quiet reflection and intense focus. The science world needs more diverse voices, not because of gender, but because of experience. We must simply stop pretending that science is a male-only domain. It’s time to let every child, regardless of gender, see themselves as capable and curious. That’s how real progress happens.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The gender gap in science is rooted in societal expectations, not innate ability. As a woman with a background in practical work and limited formal education, I observe that early exposure and encouragement are key. My personal experiences and values emphasize hands-on learning and equal opportunity. The solution lies in changing cultural narratives, starting at home and in schools, and celebrating diverse participation in science.
response_parsed.tone: grounded, practical, warm, rooted in lived experience
